facts.title: Kimchi at every meal
facts.fact: Kimchi — fermented vegetables seasoned with chili and garlic — accompanies almost every Korean meal. Families traditionally make big batches in late autumn in an event called gimjang.
facts.note: **김장** (gimjang), the communal kimchi-making, is on UNESCO's intangible heritage list.

facts.title: Rice, soup, and many side dishes
facts.fact: A Korean meal centers on rice and soup with many small shared side dishes called banchan, which are refilled for free at most restaurants.
facts.korean_en: I eat rice and side dishes.
facts.note: **하고** means "and" when joining nouns, and is very common in speech.

facts.title: Grill-it-yourself barbecue
facts.fact: At a Korean barbecue you grill meat at your own table and wrap it in lettuce with garlic, ssamjang, and rice. Meals are social and shared.
facts.note: **삼겹살** (samgyeopsal, pork belly) is the most popular cut.

facts.title: Bibimbap, the mixed rice bowl
facts.fact: Bibimbap is a bowl of warm rice topped with vegetables, meat, an egg, and red pepper paste. You **mix everything together** before eating.
facts.korean_en: I mix the bibimbap and then eat it.
facts.note: The verb **비비다** (to mix) is the root of the name 비빔밥.

facts.title: Bulgogi, sweet marinated beef
facts.fact: Bulgogi is thin beef marinated in soy sauce, sugar, garlic, and sesame oil, then grilled or pan-fried. Its name literally means **"fire meat."**

facts.title: Tteokbokki, spicy rice cakes
facts.fact: Tteokbokki is chewy rice cakes simmered in a sweet-and-spicy red sauce. It is one of Korea's most popular street snacks.
facts.note: **조금** means "a little" and softens how strong something is.

facts.title: Japchae, glass-noodle stir-fry
facts.fact: Japchae is chewy sweet-potato glass noodles stir-fried with vegetables and beef in soy sauce and sesame oil. It is a favorite dish at parties and holidays.
facts.korean_en: We eat japchae at the party.
facts.note: Glass noodles are made from **고구마** (sweet potato) starch.

facts.title: Samgyetang for hot summer days
facts.fact: Samgyetang is a whole young chicken stuffed with rice, ginseng, and jujube in a hot broth. Koreans eat it on the hottest summer days to restore energy.
facts.note: The hottest summer days are called **복날** (boknal).

facts.title: Naengmyeon, cold buckwheat noodles
facts.fact: Naengmyeon is chewy buckwheat noodles served in an icy broth or with a spicy sauce. It is a refreshing dish people crave in hot weather.
facts.note: **냉** comes from a Chinese root meaning "cold," seen in many food names.

facts.title: Jjigae, the bubbling stew
facts.fact: Jjigae is a thick, hearty stew served bubbling hot in a stone pot. Popular kinds include kimchi jjigae, soybean-paste jjigae, and soft-tofu jjigae.
facts.note: A **찌개** is thicker and saltier than a **국** (a lighter soup).

facts.title: Gimbap, the rolled rice snack
facts.fact: Gimbap is rice and fillings rolled in dried seaweed and sliced into bite-sized rounds. It is cheap, portable, and a classic picnic food.
facts.korean_en: I take gimbap to the picnic.

facts.title: Hotteok, sweet winter pancakes
facts.fact: Hotteok is a chewy fried pancake filled with melted brown sugar, cinnamon, and nuts. Street vendors sell it hot in winter.
facts.korean_en: In winter I buy and eat hotteok.
facts.note: **사 먹다** literally "buy and eat" means to eat out rather than cook.

facts.title: Makgeolli, milky rice wine
facts.fact: Makgeolli is a milky, lightly fizzy rice wine with a low alcohol content. It is often enjoyed with savory pancakes on rainy days.
facts.korean_en: On rainy days we drink makgeolli.
facts.note: **비가 오다** (literally "rain comes") is the usual way to say "it rains."

facts.title: Soju, the everyday spirit
facts.fact: Soju is a clear, inexpensive distilled spirit and Korea's most popular alcoholic drink. It is usually shared in small glasses among friends.
facts.korean_en: I pour the drink to an elder with two hands.
facts.note: Pouring for an elder with **두 손** (two hands) shows respect.

facts.title: Temple food, calm and meatless
facts.fact: Buddhist temple cuisine uses no meat and avoids strong-smelling vegetables like garlic and onion. It emphasizes seasonal, simple, plant-based dishes.
facts.korean_en: At the temple they don't eat meat.
facts.note: Placing **안** before a verb makes it negative: 안 먹어요 = "don't eat."

facts.title: Jang, the fermented pastes
facts.fact: Korean cooking relies on fermented soybean pastes called jang: doenjang (soybean paste), gochujang (red pepper paste), and ganjang (soy sauce). They give dishes their deep, savory flavor.
facts.note: The ending **-으로** here means "from / out of" a material.

facts.title: Songpyeon for the harvest holiday
facts.fact: Songpyeon are half-moon rice cakes filled with sweet sesame or beans and steamed over pine needles. Families make them together for the autumn holiday Chuseok.
facts.note: **떡** (rice cake) appears at nearly every Korean holiday and celebration.

facts.title: Seaweed soup on birthdays
facts.fact: Miyeokguk is a light soup made from seaweed. Koreans eat it on their **birthday** and mothers eat it after childbirth for nourishment.
facts.korean_en: We eat seaweed soup on birthdays.
facts.note: The time-marker **-에** (at/on) attaches to days and times, as in 생일에.

facts.title: Saying "I will eat well"
facts.fact: Before eating, Koreans often say **"jal meokgesseumnida"** (I will eat well) to thank whoever prepared or paid for the meal, and "jal meogeosseumnida" afterward.
facts.korean_en: I will eat well. (said before a meal)
facts.note: After eating, say **잘 먹었습니다** ("I ate well") to give thanks.
